title: Professional Python Data Engineer Assessment
description: Expert-level assessment for Data Engineers covering distributed computing, data modeling, ETL optimization, and pipeline orchestration.

questions.text: What is the primary difference between ETL and ELT architectures in modern data warehousing?
options: ETL is faster than ELT
ETL transforms data before loading it into the warehouse; ELT loads raw data first and leverages the warehouse's compute power for transformation
ELT is only for NoSQL databases
ETL is deprecated and no longer used
questions.explanation: ELT is popular with cloud warehouses like Snowflake or BigQuery because it allows for high-speed ingestion and scalable, SQL-based transformations later.

questions.text: In Apache Spark, what is the difference between a 'Transformation' and an 'Action'?
options: Transformations are fast; Actions are slow
Transformations are lazy (build an execution plan); Actions trigger the actual computation and return results
Actions are only for writing to disk
Transformations can only be done in SQL
questions.explanation: Spark uses lazy evaluation. A Transformation (like `map` or `filter`) doesn't run until an Action (like `collect` or `save`) is called.

questions.text: Which file format is 'columnar' and highly optimized for analytical queries in big data ecosystems?
options: CSV
JSON
Apache Parquet
XML
questions.explanation: Parquet stores data by column rather than row, allowing for efficient compression and 'predicate pushdown' (skipping irrelevant data blocks).

questions.text: What is 'Data Partitioning' in a distributed database or file system (like HDFS or S3)?
options: Deleting old data
Dividing a large dataset into smaller, manageable chunks based on a key (e.g., date or region) to improve query performance
Encrypting parts of the data
Copying data to a different cloud provider
questions.explanation: Partitioning allows the query engine to skip entire directories of data that don't match the query criteria (partition pruning).

questions.text: In Apache Airflow, what is a 'DAG'?
options: A type of database
Directed Acyclic Graph; a collection of all the tasks you want to run, organized in a way that reflects their relationships and dependencies
A Python library for math
An authentication protocol
questions.explanation: DAGs ensure that tasks run in the correct order and that there are no circular dependencies.

questions.text: What is the 'Lambda Architecture'?
options: A way to write anonymous functions
A data-processing architecture designed to handle massive quantities of data by taking advantage of both batch and stream-processing methods
A serverless-only architecture
A type of neural network
questions.explanation: Lambda balances low-latency (speed layer) with comprehensive accuracy (batch layer).

questions.text: What is 'Idempotency' in a data pipeline, and why is it critical?
options: The ability to run on any OS
The property where running a pipeline multiple times with the same input produces the same output without creating duplicates
The speed of data transfer
The ability to handle different data types
questions.explanation: If a pipeline fails halfway and is restarted, an idempotent design ensures you don't end up with duplicate records in the target table.

questions.text: What is the difference between a 'Star Schema' and a 'Snowflake Schema' in data modeling?
options: Star is for small data; Snowflake is for big data
Star has denormalized dimensions; Snowflake has normalized dimensions (where dimension tables link to other dimension tables)
Snowflake is only for the Snowflake database
Star uses NoSQL; Snowflake uses SQL
questions.explanation: Star schemas are generally faster for querying due to fewer joins, while Snowflake schemas save space through normalization.

questions.text: In Spark, what is 'Data Skew' and how can 'Salting' fix it?
options: Skew is when data is encrypted; Salting is the key
Skew is an uneven distribution of data across partitions; Salting adds a random prefix to keys to distribute them more evenly during a shuffle
Skew is a type of error; Salting is a backup method
Skew happens when the cluster is too small
questions.explanation: Skew causes some executors to work much harder than others. Salting breaks up large keys to balance the workload.

questions.text: What is 'CDC' (Change Data Capture)?
options: A virus scanning tool
A set of software design patterns used to determine and track the data that has changed so that action can be taken using the changed data
A type of data compression
A data encryption standard
questions.explanation: CDC allows you to sync databases in real-time by reading transaction logs (e.g., using Debezium) rather than bulk-querying tables.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a 'Dead Letter Queue' (DLQ)?
options: To store deleted emails
To hold messages/records that cannot be processed successfully after multiple attempts, allowing for isolation and later analysis
To speed up the network
To store old data backups
questions.explanation: DLQs prevent 'poison pills' from blocking a pipeline indefinitely while ensuring no data is lost.

questions.text: What is 'SCD Type 2' (Slowly Changing Dimension)?
options: Deleting old records
Storing the entire history of changes by creating a new row for every change with start/end dates or a version flag
Overwriting the old record with new data
Ignoring changes to dimensions
questions.explanation: SCD Type 2 is standard for auditing and historical reporting in data warehouses.

questions.text: What is 'Backfilling' in data engineering?
options: Backing up the database
The process of running a data pipeline on historical data that was either missed or needs to be recalculated
Cleaning up temporary files
Moving data to a lower-cost storage tier
questions.explanation: Backfilling is common when logic changes or when a system was down for a period.

questions.text: In a distributed system, what is the 'CAP Theorem'?
options: Consistency, Accuracy, Performance
Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance; a system can only guarantee two of these at once
Capacity, Availability, Parallelism
A rule for naming variables
questions.explanation: Data engineers must choose between Consistency and Availability when a network partition occurs.

questions.text: What is the function of the 'Broadcast Join' in Spark?
options: To join tables over a radio frequency
Sending a small table to all worker nodes to avoid a large-scale data shuffle during a join with a much larger table
Joining tables across different clouds
A way to join tables without a key
questions.explanation: Broadcast joins are much faster than shuffle-sort-merge joins because they eliminate the need to move large amounts of data across the network.

questions.text: What is 'Data Lineage'?
options: The age of the data
The lifecycle of data that includes the data's origins and where it moves over time, providing visibility into transformations
The size of the data rows
The family tree of the developers
questions.explanation: Lineage is crucial for troubleshooting, compliance (GDPR), and impact analysis when a source schema changes.

questions.text: What is an 'UPSERT' operation?
options: Deleting and then inserting data
A combination of UPDATE and INSERT; it updates a record if it exists, otherwise inserts a new one
Converting data to uppercase
Uploading data to a server
questions.explanation: Upserts are vital for maintaining the current state of records without creating duplicates.

questions.text: Which tool is best for 'Schema Registry' in a Kafka ecosystem?
options: PostgreSQL
Confluent Schema Registry
Airflow
Excel
questions.explanation: A Schema Registry ensures that producers and consumers agree on the data format (usually Avro or Protobuf), preventing pipeline breaks.

questions.text: What is the purpose of 'Checkpointing' in Spark Streaming?
options: To check the code for errors
Saving the state of a streaming query to reliable storage (like HDFS/S3) so it can recover from failures
To limit the speed of data ingestion
To count the number of records
questions.explanation: Without checkpointing, a streaming job would have to restart from the beginning of the stream upon failure.

questions.text: In Data Warehousing, what is an 'OLAP' system vs an 'OLTP' system?
options: OLAP is for transactions; OLTP is for analytics
OLAP is for complex analytical queries (Read-heavy); OLTP is for day-to-day transactional processing (Write-heavy)
There is no difference
OLAP is for local data; OLTP is for cloud data
questions.explanation: Data Warehouses (OLAP) are optimized for aggregation and scanning, while relational databases (OLTP) are optimized for row-level updates.

questions.text: What is 'Windowing' in stream processing?
options: Using a computer with Windows OS
Grouping stream data into finite chunks based on time or count to perform aggregations (e.g., average price over the last 5 minutes)
Creating a popup window for the user
Viewing data in a text editor
questions.explanation: Tumbling, Sliding, and Session windows are common techniques in Flink, Spark Streaming, or Kafka Streams.

questions.text: What is the 'Medallion Architecture' in Databricks/Delta Lake?
options: Bronze, Silver, and Gold layers representing raw, cleaned, and business-ready data
A way to award developers
A type of data encryption
A 3-server setup
questions.explanation: This architecture provides a structured way to improve data quality as it moves through the pipeline.

questions.text: What is 'Small File Problem' in HDFS/S3, and how do you solve it?
options: Small files are too fast; use larger files
Too many small files cause overhead in metadata management; solve by compacting/coalescing data into larger files
Small files take up too much disk space
Small files cannot be encrypted
questions.explanation: Managing metadata for millions of small files overwhelms the NameNode (HDFS) or slows down listing operations (S3).

questions.text: What does 'ACID' stand for in database systems?
options: Accuracy, Completeness, Integrity, Durability
Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability
Availability, Control, Identity, Distribution
None of the above
questions.explanation: ACID properties ensure that database transactions are processed reliably.

questions.text: What is 'Schema Evolution'?
options: Upgrading the database version
The ability of a data system to handle changes in data structure (like adding a column) over time without breaking downstream consumers
The process of deleting old tables
Automated documentation generation
questions.explanation: Formats like Avro and Parquet support schema evolution, allowing for backward and forward compatibility.

questions.text: In Airflow, what is an 'Operator'?
options: A person who runs the code
A template for a single task in a DAG (e.g., PythonOperator, BashOperator, S3ToRedshiftOperator)
A mathematical symbol
A data type
questions.explanation: Operators define what actually gets done (executing Python, moving files, etc.).

questions.text: What is 'Predicate Pushdown'?
options: Moving data to a different table
An optimization where filtering logic is pushed as close to the data source as possible to reduce the amount of data read from disk
A way to sort data
Deleting data that doesn't match a condition
questions.explanation: If you only need rows where `year=2023`, predicate pushdown ensures you don't even read the data for other years into memory.

questions.text: What is the difference between 'Batch' and 'Micro-batch' processing?
options: Batch is for SQL; Micro-batch is for NoSQL
Batch processes large volumes at scheduled intervals; Micro-batch (like Spark Streaming) processes small batches of data in near real-time
Micro-batch is always faster
Batch is deprecated
questions.explanation: Micro-batching bridges the gap between traditional batching and true record-at-a-time streaming.

questions.text: What is 'Data Virtualization'?
options: Creating a virtual machine for data
A data management approach that allows an application to retrieve and manipulate data without knowing the technical details or location of the data
Visualizing data in VR
Simulating data for testing
questions.explanation: Tools like Trino or Dremio allow you to query data across S3, Postgres, and Mongo as if they were a single database.

questions.text: What is the purpose of 'Z-Ordering' (or multi-dimensional clustering)?
options: To sort data alphabetically
To co-locate related information in the same set of files to improve query performance for multiple dimensions
To encrypt data in 3D
To manage the order of execution in Airflow
questions.explanation: Z-Ordering is an advanced data skipping technique used in Delta Lake and iceberg to speed up queries with multiple filters.
